summary: Tractate Yoma daf 83a covers: 'The wicked estranged from the womb': R. Yochanan on innate evil inclination; An ill person fed on YK by expert judgment; If an ill person needs to eat: feed on expert opinion or on the patient's own request?; Mishna: one seized with hungry frenzy (bulmos) - feed even non-kosher to revive; The Sages' ruling about bulmos: what to feed and why non-kosher is permitted.

display.whats: 'The wicked estranged from the womb': R. Yochanan on innate evil inclination
display.hint: R. Yochanan interprets 'the wicked estranged from the womb' as referring to the innate evil inclination present from birth.

learning.summary: R. Yochanan interprets 'the wicked estranged from the womb' as referring to the innate evil inclination present from birth.
learning.ahaMoment: Evil inclination from the womb: the Torah acknowledges that the struggle with sin begins before we are even conscious of it.
learning.memoryAnchor: Evil inclination from the womb: the struggle begins before consciousness.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about 'the wicked estranged from the womb': r. yochanan on innate ?

argumentFlow.label: The child's nature foretold by the verse
argumentFlow.speaker: R. Yochanan
argumentFlow.text: Continuing the prior story, the Gemara applies 'the wicked are estranged from the womb' (Psalms 58:4): the child's character was already set in the womb. Indeed, the child born was Shabbetai the hoarder, who stockpiled fruit in famine years to inflate prices at the expense of the poor.

theological: Evil inclination from the womb: the Torah acknowledges that the struggle with si
narrative: 'The wicked estranged from the womb': R. Yochanan on innate

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of evil inclination context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: The verse means a person's wickedness is fixed and unchangeable from birth.
misconceptions.correction: The verse is applied narratively to a particular figure whose nature was foreshadowed; it is not a general doctrine that character cannot change.

visualizableElements.name: 'The wicked estranged from the womb': R. Yochanan

quizSeeds.question: What does the verse 'the wicked are estranged from the womb' illustrate in this story?
quizSeeds.answer: That the craving woman's child, who became Shabbetai the food-hoarder, already bore his wicked nature from the womb, matching her failure to be settled by the Yom Kippur reminder.

quizSeeds.question: What was Shabbetai's wrongdoing?
quizSeeds.answer: He hoarded fruit during famine years in order to drive up prices and profit at the expense of the poor.

sugyot.finalRuling: R. Yochanan interprets 'the wicked estranged from the womb' as referring to the innate evil inclination present from birth.

display.whats: An ill person fed on YK by expert judgment
display.hint: An ill person on Yom Kippur may be fed based on expert (physician) judgment, even without the patient's request.

learning.summary: An ill person on Yom Kippur may be fed based on expert (physician) judgment, even without the patient's request.
learning.ahaMoment: Expert judgment for the ill on YK: we trust medicine. The physician's assessment is enough to override the fast - we do not demand the patient ask for it.
learning.memoryAnchor: Expert judgment for the ill: medicine is trusted; the patient need not ask.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about an ill person fed on yk by expert judgment?

argumentFlow.label: Listen to the ill person over the doctor
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yannai: if a sick person says he must eat and the doctor says he need not, one feeds the sick person, for 'the heart knows the bitterness of its soul' (Proverbs 14:10) - he feels his own pain that the doctor cannot measure.

argumentFlow.label: When the doctor overrides the patient
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: In the reverse case, where the doctor says the patient needs food but the patient says he does not, one listens to the doctor, because illness-induced confusion has clouded the patient's judgment so he cannot gauge his own need.

theological: Expert judgment for the ill on YK: we trust medicine. The physician's assessment
narrative: An ill person fed on YK by expert judgment

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of ill person context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: In a medical dispute on Yom Kippur, the doctor's expert opinion always prevails.
misconceptions.correction: When the patient says he needs to eat, his own sense of danger prevails over the doctor; the doctor prevails only when the patient denies a need the doctor identifies.

visualizableElements.name: An ill person fed on YK by expert judgment

quizSeeds.question: Why is the patient believed over the doctor when he says he must eat?
quizSeeds.answer: Because 'the heart knows the bitterness of its soul' - he directly feels his own weakness and pain, which the doctor cannot perceive from outside.

quizSeeds.question: Why is the doctor believed over the patient when the patient says he does not need to eat?
quizSeeds.answer: Because illness can cloud the patient's judgment with confusion, so he may fail to recognize his own need, and we err toward saving life.

sugyot.finalRuling: An ill person on Yom Kippur may be fed based on expert (physician) judgment, even without the patient's request.

display.whats: If an ill person needs to eat: feed on expert opinion or on the patient's own request?
display.hint: The Gemara debates: if an ill person needs to eat on YK, do we rely on the physician's assessment or does the patient's own statement of need override?

learning.summary: The Gemara debates: if an ill person needs to eat on YK, do we rely on the physician's assessment or does the patient's own statement of need override?
learning.ahaMoment: Patient's own request vs. expert opinion: the more lenient standard wins. If either says the person needs to eat, we feed them.
learning.memoryAnchor: Patient or expert: whoever says they need to eat - we feed them. More lenient wins.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about if an ill person needs to eat: feed on expert opinion or on ?

argumentFlow.label: Mishnah seems to require experts, against Rabbi Yannai
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah's 'one feeds him by the advice of experts' implies one does not feed on the patient's own word, and seems to require several experts - both points contradicting Rabbi Yannai.

argumentFlow.label: Narrowing the case
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara narrows it: the Mishnah deals with a patient who says he does not need food, so experts are consulted. Why not one expert, as Rabbi Yannai allows? Because here a third expert says he does not need to eat, so two are needed to outweigh him.

argumentFlow.label: Two against one in life-and-death assessment
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Even though this is uncertain danger where we are normally lenient, the ruling is needed where two experts oppose one who says the patient needs food: in assessing danger we follow the experts who would feed him.

argumentFlow.label: Assessment is not testimony
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Where two experts say feed and two plus the patient say do not, one still feeds him. Rav Safra's rule that 'two witnesses are like a hundred' applies to testimony, not to assessing life-or-death uncertainty, where any reasonable view that feeds is followed.

argumentFlow.label: Majority does not govern danger
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Following the majority governs monetary and evidentiary matters; in uncertain danger to life it does not, so even against a numerical majority the patient is fed.

argumentFlow.label: Mar bar Rav Ashi: the patient's word is decisive
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Mar bar Rav Ashi: whenever the patient says he must eat, even a hundred experts saying otherwise are overruled, for 'the heart knows the bitterness of its soul.'

argumentFlow.label: Reading the Mishnah's two clauses together
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah's later clause - if no experts, feed by his own word - might imply the first clause overrides his word; the Gemara answers the Mishnah is elliptical: experts are followed only when the patient himself says he does not need to eat.

theological: Patient's own request vs. expert opinion: the more lenient standard wins. If eit
narrative: If an ill person needs to eat: feed on expert opinion or on

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of ill person context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Assessing whether a sick person may eat works like court testimony, where two witnesses settle the matter.
misconceptions.correction: Rav Safra's 'two are like a hundred' rule is for testimony; danger to life is not testimony, so we rule leniently to preserve life regardless of the count of opinions.

visualizableElements.name: If an ill person needs to eat: feed on expert opin

quizSeeds.question: Why doesn't the principle 'follow the majority' decide cases of feeding an ill person on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Majority rule governs monetary and evidentiary matters; uncertain danger to life is decided leniently toward preserving life, so the patient is fed even against a numerical majority of experts.

quizSeeds.question: How does Mar bar Rav Ashi rule when the patient insists he must eat against a hundred experts?
quizSeeds.answer: The patient is fed, because 'the heart knows the bitterness of its soul' - his own sense of danger overrides any number of experts who disagree.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara debates: if an ill person needs to eat on YK, do we rely on the physician's assessment or does the patient's own statement of need override

display.whats: Mishna: one seized with hungry frenzy (bulmos) - feed even non-kosher to revive
display.hint: The Mishna: someone seized with an extreme hunger attack (bulmos) is fed even non-kosher food to save their life - in emergency, non-kosher overrides YK.

learning.summary: The Mishna: someone seized with an extreme hunger attack (bulmos) is fed even non-kosher food to save their life - in emergency, non-kosher overrides YK.
learning.ahaMoment: Bulmos and non-kosher food: the hierarchy of prohibitions is clear - life comes first, then kashrut, then YK. Emergency overrides all other categories.
learning.memoryAnchor: Bulmos gets non-kosher: emergency collapses all the usual hierarchies.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about mishna: one seized with hungry frenzy (bulmos) - feed even n?

argumentFlow.label: Mishnah: bulmos, mad dog, throat pain, rockslide
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: One seized by the ravenous illness bulmos may be fed even impure foods until his sight returns. One bitten by a mad dog is not fed the lobe of its liver - the Rabbis deem it useless, though Rabbi Matya ben Charash permits it.

argumentFlow.label: Throat pain and medicine on Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Matya ben Charash also rules that one with throat pain may be given medicine in his mouth on Shabbat, since internal pain may be life-threatening and uncertain danger to life overrides Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: Rockslide: clear the debris despite every doubt
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: If a rockslide may have buried someone, one clears it despite uncertainty whether anyone is there, whether he is alive, or whether he is a Jew or gentile; if found alive one continues, and if dead one leaves him.

argumentFlow.label: Recognizing recovery from bulmos by sight
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The Sages: one knows the bulmos sufferer's eyes have recovered when he can tell good food from bad, since under bulmos he eats indiscriminately. Abaye: the sign is that he can again distinguish tastes, even in the dark.

theological: Bulmos and non-kosher food: the hierarchy of prohibitions is clear - life comes
narrative: Mishna: one seized with hungry frenzy (bulmos) - feed even n

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: One clears a rockslide on Shabbat only after confirming a Jew is alive beneath it.
misconceptions.correction: Because uncertain danger to life overrides Shabbat, one clears the debris immediately despite doubt about whether anyone, let alone a living Jew, is there.

visualizableElements.name: Mishna: one seized with hungry frenzy (bulmos) - f

quizSeeds.question: Why does uncertainty about who is buried under a rockslide not prevent clearing it on Shabbat?
quizSeeds.answer: Uncertain danger to life overrides Shabbat, so one clears the debris even when it is unknown whether anyone is there, whether he is alive, or whether he is a Jew.

quizSeeds.question: How does one recognize that a bulmos sufferer has recovered?
quizSeeds.answer: When his vision returns he can again distinguish good food from bad, or per Abaye distinguish tastes even in the dark, whereas under bulmos he eats indiscriminately.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Mishna: someone seized with an extreme hunger attack (bulmos) is fed even non-kosher food to save their life - in emergency, non-kosher overrides

display.whats: The Sages' ruling about bulmos: what to feed and why non-kosher is permitted
display.hint: The Sages explain why non-kosher is the first thing to give the person with bulmos: sometimes it is more readily available or quickly effective.

learning.summary: The Sages explain why non-kosher is the first thing to give the person with bulmos: sometimes it is more readily available or quickly effective.
learning.ahaMoment: What to give someone with bulmos: the fastest, most effective remedy wins, even if it would normally be forbidden.
learning.memoryAnchor: Feed bulmos with what works fastest: effectiveness, not piety, governs the emergency.
learning.openQuestion: What is the deeper significance of the ruling about the sages' ruling about bulmos: what to feed and why non-kos?

argumentFlow.label: Feed the least-prohibited food first
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A bulmos sufferer who must eat forbidden food is fed the least severe prohibition first. Given untithed produce and an animal carcass, one feeds the carcass, since untithed produce carries death by Heaven while non-kosher meat carries only lashes.

argumentFlow.label: Untithed produce versus teruma dispute
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Between untithed produce and teruma the tannaim dispute which to feed. Rabba notes that where one can simply tithe the produce to make it permitted, all agree to do so, even on Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: Why each side ranks them differently
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: They disagree only where tithing is impossible. One Sage holds untithed produce is more severe because it is forbidden to everyone, whereas teruma is at least fit for a priest; the other holds teruma is more severe because non-priests may never eat it.

theological: What to give someone with bulmos: the fastest, most effective remedy wins, even
narrative: The Sages' ruling about bulmos: what to feed and why non-kos

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Any forbidden food may be fed freely to a person in danger.
misconceptions.correction: Even when forbidden food must be given, one minimizes the violation by feeding the least severe prohibition first and, where possible, tithing produce to make it permitted.

visualizableElements.name: The Sages' ruling about bulmos: what to feed and w

quizSeeds.question: When a bulmos sufferer must eat forbidden food, in what order is it given?
quizSeeds.answer: From the least severe prohibition upward - for example a non-kosher carcass, which carries only lashes, before untithed produce, which carries death by Heaven.

quizSeeds.question: Where do the tannaim actually disagree about untithed produce versus teruma?
quizSeeds.answer: Only where tithing is impossible; one holds untithed produce is worse since it is forbidden to all, the other holds teruma is worse since non-priests may never eat it.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Sages explain why non-kosher is the first thing to give the person with bulmos: sometimes it is more readily available or quickly effective.

rashiTranslations.he: זורו רשעים מרחם. נעשו זרים ונתנכרו לאביהם שבשמים: אצר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: R. Yochanan on the evil inclination present from birth.

rashiTranslations.he: פירי. להפקיע שערים ותניא (ב"ב דף צ:) אין אוצרין פירות בארץ
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: an ill person may be fed on YK based on expert judgment.

rashiTranslations.he: ישראל וכל דבר שהוא חיי נפש לפי שמפקיעין את השערים:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the Mishna's case of someone seized with ravenous hunger (bulmos).

rashiTranslations.he: תונבא. שטות אשטורדישו"ן מחמת חוליו: על פי בקיאין. תרי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: non-kosher food may be given to save someone from bulmos.

rashiTranslations.he: ע"פ בקי לא וע"פ עצמו לא תיובתא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the principle: even forbidden food is permitted to save life.

rashiTranslations.he: דר' ינאי בתרוייהו: הכא במאי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: עסקינן. דאמר חולה לא צריך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: ואשמועינן מתני' דשומעין לרופאין:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: וניספו ליה על פי בקי. דהא באחד
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: נמי אמר ר' ינאי דשומעין לרופא:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: ואע"ג דאמר רב ספרא. דהא דאמור
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: רבנן תרי כמאה ומאה כתרי לענין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: עדות הוא דאמור אבל לענין אומדנא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: בתר דעות אזלינן אשמעי' במתני'
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 83a.

rashiTranslations.he: דכי אמרינן זיל בתר דעות באומדנא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דממונא כגון בשומא ששמין לבעל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: חוב אבל באומדנא דחולה אף על גב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דרובא אמרי לא צריך כיון דאיכא תרי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דאמרי צריך ספק נפשות להקל: מכלל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דרישא דאמר צריך. ואפ"ה קתני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מתני' ע"פ בקיאין אין ע"פ עצמו לא:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: במה דברים אמורים. דמאכילין אותו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ע"פ בקיאין כלומר דבעינן בקיאין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דאמר לא צריך ואמרי בקיאין צריך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מאכילין אותו אבל אמר צריך אני אם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אין שם בקיאין תרי דאמרי לא צריך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אלא חד מאכילין אותו ע"פ עצמו כר'
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ינאי דאמר שומעין לחולה: מר בר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: רב אשי אמר. הך סיפא לא תפרש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: הכי דמשמע דהיכא דאמר צריך אני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ואיכא תרי דאמרי לא צריך לא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: צייתינן ליה הא לא אמרי' דהיכא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: דאמר צריך אני ואפי' איכא מאה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: דאמרי לא צריך צייתינן ליה מאי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: טעמא לב יודע מרת נפשו: הכי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: גר' תנן אם אין שם בקיאין מאכילין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אותו על פי עצמו טעמא דליכא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בקיאין הא איכא בקיאין לא הכי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: קאמר במה דברים אמורים דאמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לא צריך אבל אמר צריך אני אין שם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בקיאין כלל מאכילין אותו וכו'. אין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שם בקיאין כלומר אין בקיאותן כלום:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: מתני' מי שאחזו בולמוס. חולי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: האוחז מחמת רעבון עיניו כהות והוא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi defines bulmos: it is a sickness that seizes a person from extreme hunger, causing his eyes to dim.

rashiTranslations.he: מסוכן למות וכשמראיתו חוזרת בידוע
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues: the person is in danger of dying, and when his vision returns it is known that he has recovered.

rashiTranslations.he: שנתרפא: כלב שוטה. מפרש בגמ':
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi closes the bulmos definition - 'when he has been healed' - then opens a new lemma: 'kelev shoteh' (a rabid dog), noting it is explained in the Gemara.

rashiTranslations.he: אין מאכילין מחצר כבד שלו. ואע"פ
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses the Mishna's ruling: we do not feed the bite victim the lobe of the dog's liver. Rashi continues: even though.

rashiTranslations.he: שנוהגין הרופאים ברפואה זו אינה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues: physicians practice this remedy, it is not a complete enough cure.

rashiTranslations.he: רפואה גמורה להתיר לו איסור
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues the clause: not a complete remedy sufficient to permit him to eat.

rashiTranslations.he: בהמה טמאה על כך: ור' מתיא בן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi completes the clause: a Torah-prohibited animal for this purpose. Then Rashi opens: 'and Rabbi Matya ben' - introducing his dissenting opinion.

rashiTranslations.he: חרש מתיר. קסבר רפואה גמורה היא: גמ' אמר אביי ובטעמא. משיודע להבחין בין טעם תבשיל יפה לטעם תבשיל רע: מאכילין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains: Rabbi Matya ben Charash permits it because he holds it is a complete cure. Then Rashi cites the Gemara - Abaye's reasoning: recovery is known from when he can again distinguish between the taste of a good dish and the taste of a bad one. Rashi then opens: 'we feed him the lightest' - if no permitted food is available and various prohibited foods are before us, we feed him the least severe.

rashiTranslations.he: אותו הקל הקל. אם אין לנו דברים מותרים כדי צרכו ויש לפנינו מיני איסורין מאכילין אותו הקל הקל שבהם: טבל ושביעית מאכילין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains: 'tevel and shevi'it - we feed him shevi'it' - after the time of bi'ur, since tevel carries a heavenly death penalty while shevi'it carries only a positive commandment prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: אותו שביעית. לאחר זמן הביעור שהטבל במיתה בידי שמים והשביעית בעשה: בדאפשר בחולין. שיש די בחולין של טבל זה להאכילו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains 'when chullin is possible': meaning there is enough non-sacred produce from this tevel to feed him after terumah is separated. Then Rashi explains the unanimous rule: everyone agrees we fix it and feed him chullin, not tevel or terumah.

rashiTranslations.he: לאחר שתינטל תרומתו: דכולי עלמא לא פליגי דמתקנינן ליה ומספינן ליה. חולין ואין מאכילין אותו לא טבל ולא תרומה: כי פליגי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains 'where they disagree': when chullin is not possible unless he eats the whole produce. The ruling is to feed him the tevel as-is, without separating terumah to give him each portion separately.

rashiTranslations.he: דלא אפשר בחולין. אלא אם כן אוכל את כולו וקאמר מאכילין אותו הטבל כמות שהוא ולא יפרישו ממנו תרומתו להאכילו תרומה לעצמה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi continues: and chullin separately. Ben Teima says it is better to separate the terumah and feed him each portion on its own, rather than feed him the tevel as-is.

rashiTranslations.he: וחולין לעצמן ובן תימא אומר מוטב שיפרישו תרומתו ויאכילוהו כל אחד לעצמו ולא יאכילוהו טבל כמות שהוא: מר סבר טבל חמור.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the first opinion: tevel (untithed produce) is more severe because it is not fit even for kohanim; therefore it is better to separate terumah and let each person eat his portion individually, rather than feed the sick person tevel as-is.

rashiTranslations.he: שאפי' לכהנים אינו ראוי: ומר סבר תרומה חמורה. שאין לה היתר אבל טבל אפשר דמתקן להו וחולין יהו ראוין לכל אדם:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opposing view: the other sage holds terumah is more severe because it has no corrective fix, while tevel can be remedied by tithing, after which the produce becomes ordinary chullin permitted to everyone.

rashiTranslations.he: אפשר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi opens the comment on 'it is possible' (efshar), continuing the discussion of how tevel can be corrected while terumah cannot.
